description: This figure presents Vickers hardness (VHN) data for three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels—28Mn0Ni (Ni-free), 28Mn5Ni (high Mn with Ni), and 15Mn5Ni (lower Mn with Ni)—processed via cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment. Subfigure (a) plots hardness versus percentage reduction in thickness during cold rolling (0% to 50%). All alloys exhibit substantial work hardening: initial hot-rolled hardness values of ~250 VHN (28Mn0Ni), ~260 VHN (28Mn5Ni), and ~380 VHN (15Mn5Ni) converge to similar values of ~430–460 VHN after 50% reduction, indicating comparable work-hardening capacity despite different initial microstructures. Subfigure (b) shows hardness as a function of heat treatment temperature (873 K to 1273 K) for 50% cold-rolled samples annealed for 1 hour. Hardness decreases with increasing temperature for all alloys, but the softening behavior differs significantly. The Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy displays the steepest decline, dropping from ~430 VHN at 873 K to ~190 VHN at 1273 K. In contrast, 15Mn5Ni maintains the highest hardness across the temperature range (~500 VHN at 873 K to ~300 VHN at 1273 K), with 28Mn5Ni showing intermediate retention. These trends suggest that Ni addition and lower Mn content enhance high-temperature hardness stability, likely due to the presence of B2 intermetallic particles and κ-carbides that retard recrystallization and grain growth during annealing.
